era_context: The Late Bronze Age, a period of interconnected palace-based civilisations across the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East. The New Kingdom of Egypt under the 18th Dynasty (Thutmosid era) was at its height; the Hittite Empire dominated Anatolia; Mycenaean Greece was in the Late Helladic IIIA period; the Shang Dynasty ruled China; and the Kassite dynasty controlled Babylonia. This was an era of intensive diplomatic correspondence, trade networks, and military competition between great powers.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited archaeological record for this period)
Interior Americas (no written records; archaeological evidence sparse)

events.title: Reign of Amenhotep III at its zenith; construction of Luxor Temple continues
events.description: Amenhotep III (r. c. 1390–1352 BCE) was at the height of his power and prestige, overseeing a period of unprecedented Egyptian prosperity and artistic achievement. The construction of the Luxor Temple (Ipet-resyt), dedicated to Amun-Ra, was ongoing during this period. His reign was marked by extensive diplomatic correspondence with other great powers (Hittites, Mitanni, Babylonians), evidenced by the Amarna Letters archive. The pharaoh commissioned numerous monuments and maintained Egypt's imperial dominance in Nubia and the Levant.

sources.name: Thutmose III and Amenhotep III temple records

events.certainty_note: Amenhotep III's reign is well-attested through contemporary inscriptions, temple reliefs, and diplomatic correspondence. His regnal dates are secure within ±2 years.
cross_references: Precedes -1352-001 (Amenhotep III's death and Akhenaten's accession)

events.title: Hittite Empire under Šuppiluliuma I consolidates power in Anatolia and Syria
events.description: Šuppiluliuma I (r. c. 1355–1322 BCE) was in the early phase of his reign, during which he would become one of the greatest Hittite kings. By 1350 BCE, he had begun military campaigns to expand Hittite control over Syria and challenge Egyptian dominance in the region. His reign marked the beginning of the Hittite Empire's rise to great-power status, rivalling Egypt and Babylonia. The Hittites' use of iron-working technology and chariot warfare gave them military advantages.

sources.name: Hittite royal inscriptions and annals (clay tablets from Boğazköy)
Amarna Letters (Egyptian diplomatic correspondence mentioning Hittite activities)

events.certainty_note: Šuppiluliuma I's regnal dates are debated (some scholars place his reign c. 1355–1322 BCE, others c. 1344–1322 BCE). The chronology of his early campaigns is approximate.

events.title: Shang Dynasty in China: reign of late Shang kings; oracle bone divination practices
events.description: The Shang Dynasty (c. 1600–1046 BCE) was in its later phase. By 1350 BCE, the Shang court was engaged in ritual divination using oracle bones (scapulae and plastrons of animals, inscribed with questions and heated to produce cracks interpreted as answers). These practices are extensively documented in the oracle bone script, one of the earliest forms of Chinese writing. The Shang maintained a sophisticated bronze-working technology and a hierarchical court system centred on the king's ritual authority.
key_figures: Late Shang kings (specific ruler uncertain for this year)
events.certainty_note: The Shang Dynasty's chronology is debated; traditional dates place it c. 1600–1046 BCE, but some scholars argue for a later start date (c. 1250 BCE). The specific ruler in 1350 BCE is uncertain. Oracle bone practices are confirmed but cannot be precisely dated to individual years.
cross_references: Part of broader Shang Dynasty period (-1600 to -1046)

events.title: Mycenaean Greece in Late Helladic IIIA: palace-based societies and Linear B administration
events.description: Mycenaean palace societies in Greece were at their height during Late Helladic IIIA (c. 1425–1190 BCE). By 1350 BCE, major palace centres such as Mycenae, Tiryns, and Pylos were flourishing, with complex administrative systems recorded in Linear B script (an early form of Greek). These palaces controlled extensive territories and engaged in trade across the Mediterranean. The period is marked by monumental architecture (tholos tombs, citadel walls) and evidence of a warrior aristocracy.
key_figures: Mycenaean palace rulers (names unknown; Linear B records refer to 'wanax' or king)

sources.name: Linear B tablets from Mycenae, Tiryns, and Pylos
Archaeological evidence from palace excavations (pottery, architecture, artefacts)

events.certainty_note: Late Helladic IIIA chronology is well-established through pottery typology and radiocarbon dating. The existence of palace-based societies and Linear B administration is confirmed by extensive archaeological evidence.

events.title: Kassite Babylonia under Kurigalzu II: administrative and military consolidation
events.description: The Kassite dynasty ruled Babylonia from c. 1595–1155 BCE. By 1350 BCE, Kurigalzu II (r. c. 1345–1324 BCE) was in the early years of his reign, a period of administrative consolidation and military campaigns. The Kassites maintained Babylonian cultural traditions while introducing their own administrative practices. Kurigalzu II is known for his military activities against the Elamites and his construction projects, including the city of Dur-Kurigalzu.

sources.name: Babylonian king lists and chronicles (later compilations)

events.certainty_note: Kurigalzu II's regnal dates are estimated at c. 1345–1324 BCE based on king lists and synchronisms with Egyptian chronology. The dating is approximate (±5–10 years).

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of the Shang Dynasty remains contested; some scholars argue for a significantly later chronology (c. 1250 BCE start), which would affect the attribution of oracle bone practices to 1350 BCE. Additionally, the exact regnal dates of Amenhotep III, Šuppiluliuma I, and Kurigalzu II are subject to ongoing scholarly debate, with some chronologies placing them ±5–10 years differently. The 'High Chronology' and 'Low Chronology' systems for the Late Bronze Age produce different absolute dates for these rulers.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1350 BCE is heavily weighted toward Egypt, the Hittite Empire, and Mycenaean Greece, where written records and extensive archaeological evidence exist. Babylonia is well-documented through king lists and inscriptions, though these are often later compilations. China's Shang Dynasty is known primarily through oracle bones and bronze inscriptions, but the chronology remains debated. Sub-Saharan Africa, the interior Americas, and much of northern Europe are essentially absent from the written record for this period, though archaeological evidence exists. The surviving sources are predominantly elite-focused (royal inscriptions, palace records, diplomatic correspondence) and may not reflect broader social or economic conditions. Egyptian sources, in particular, are shaped by royal ideology and may overstate Egyptian power and influence.

graph_edges.note: Amenhotep III's reign ends with his death c. 1352 BCE; succeeded by Akhenaten
Šuppiluliuma I's early consolidation leads to major Syrian campaigns in the 1340s BCE
Amenhotep III and Šuppiluliuma I were contemporaries, competing for influence in Syria
Shang Dynasty in China and Mycenaean Greece were contemporaneous, though no direct contact
